author: @55Ramius
text: My earliest memories of my dad, who died when I was 7, was a visit to a scrapyard he worked at. I remember finding a broken battery-operated bulldozer and we worked on it until it ran again. Thrilled me a lot. Back then we had next to no money and toys only showed up on Christmas. Most not new.  The scrapyard crane had an electromagnet on it for loading scrap and he would give us a ride on the magnet, only a foot off the ground but was cool. This was in 1961.  For the rest of my life, I would get a bit excited at what may lay in someone's junk heap that I could mess with.  One man's junk is another man's treasure is true..   : )

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: you were one l lucky boy! I am in envy of your childhood

author: @DoubleBob
text:
Wow! This sounds cool. What should I be searching to learn more about this?
I have never done it and I don't want to be hornswoggled when I try. Like what are appropriate prices? Do I ask in advance or just go there? Do I pay for entering? What about electronics?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: look up a few local yards and go there or phone - it's a very much play it by ear to be honest - explain who you are what you want. You shouldn't pay to enter if they let you you will pay scrap price or a little bit over for the favour they are doing you. There will be all kinds of stuff there that's for sure including electronics - but it is a scrap yard so what is there is there and it will change - but. mostly it is a people thing - be nice, be open and be kind and you should do okay

replies.author: @DoubleBob
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Thanks! I'll do.

author: @wethepeople1776x
text:
What a score!! That’s absolutely amazing!!, all the new resources you’ll be able to find and gather for new projects or revisiting old ones.
A great way to show appreciation to your new found chums (in my mind) would be to build them something from things you gathered from their yard that they could use at work (a self renewable electric source that would like recharge their phones) or a mechanized center piece of some sort (like a self renewable that would sound an alarm and do something marking their break periods). 
I don’t know mate, but my gears would be turning to create something from their scrap (as a thanks and show of appreciation) that they couldn’t forget and I’ll be remembered for many years to come. 😜

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is an awesome idea - I am going to have a think and suggest it to them - thanks for the suggestion mate - I love it

author: @kilokilos
text: Water has a memory. It is a hologram of the past. See Victor Shauburger. The magnets are to wipe the memory of the water similar to wiping a hard drive. Water are treated rather badly in processing plants.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: ahh - cheers mate

replies.author: @kilokilos

author: @allanwood3562
text: Brilliant. I have spent many happy times tip ratting (the good old days) but the last couple of days I've been removing window winder motors from a few car doors. I recovered the glass too that will take a bit nutting out to reuse although I can feel a sculpture project coming on :)

replies.author: @norfolknchance.500
replies.text: Look at window wiper motors, they are incredibly useful and powerful, with high torque for such a small motor, its surprising really, a multitude of uses!

replies.author: @allanwood3562
replies.text: @norfolknchance.500 Yes they are indeed. I've harvested them already :) Good call.

replies.author: @norfolknchance.500
replies.text: @allanwood3562 👍👌

replies.author: @norfolknchance.500
replies.text:
Not familiar with your term "nutting out"? 
I'm intriuged as to what that might be, and how you use the vehicle body glass in sculptures,  if you don't mind me being nosey!? 🤓✌

replies.author: @allanwood3562
replies.text:
@norfolknchance.500 To 'nut' is to figure out with the 'nut' being one's head. 
As for the glass, I haven't got there yet but I do have a pretty large butterfly and a dragonfly planned. Sometimes I just need to start and feel my way along.

author: @putteslaintxtbks5166
text: I have seen stuff at are local scrap yard that made my mouth water, but it was always at the wrong time, like when moving and trying to have less stuff. I've been wanting to get stuff for alot of projects and think most scrap yards will sell either for the metal price they payed or a bit more, but some wouldn't let you run around in them for a liability reasons. Last time I was there they had a whole row of like 100 gallon stainless steel nitrogen tanks. I could invision about a dozen thing I could do with one of them in about 30 seconds.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: wow - I am drooling myself lol

author: @twestgard2
text: I absolutely LOVE my local scrap yard. I’ve gotten so many useful things there, including expensive tools that people just throw away. Right now I’m refurbishing a huge sandblasting cabinet I got for $60. I’ll probably spend a couple hundred bucks on new fittings but in the end I’ll have the nicest, biggest sandblasting cabinet I’ve ever seen.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it is truly amazing what you can get there Stuart offered me two 4 ton transformers - I just could move them or I would have had them!

author: @saiello2061
text: I used to love car scrapyard scavenging. Also a local old school engineering company allowed me to wander unattended around their premises for scrap metal and weigh my bits on a mechanical scale. Chatting with the old boys was always a laugh. Then the laws changed and H&S tightened up which meant it was too risky for these premises to allow the general public in. The only scrapyards that might let you in now are outside the metroplitan areas where they might be more willing to bend the rules.... 😉

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: to be honest mate H&S gets blamed a lot and is often used an excuse but H&S is a dual responsibility - I think the real reason is insurance - some folks have sued because they were injured and injured when it was their fault - so insurance went up - H&S rules don't stop you doing anything but insurance will. So- to me - if some folks accepted responsibility for themselves we wouldn't have this situation. But it is easy to blame someone else for our own stupidity - especially if we might get a few dollars for it.

author: @mikegb1969
text: Pleased for you Rob but some wont let you mooch h&s. sad i know.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: oh he did go through the H&S mate - but the real problem is - not everyone is good - some folks would sue if they got injured in a yard - H&S is often used as an excuse to help prevent that - what is sad here is that there are some who try to take advantage - ah well - we soldier on mate

author: @you-know-who.
text: It's good to see you found new friends willing to help mate. I look forward to seeing what comes of a hat free junk. Make sure you fill them  gas canisters with water before you even think about getting the grinder out.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - thanks for the tip mate

replies.author: @norfolknchance.500
replies.text: Yep, defo purge with water first! ..... No big bangs then! Haha!

author: @stevetobias4890
text: I have found throughout my life that kindness is infectious among decent people. The times I have received really cool stuff cheap (or free) is amazing. It's always worthwhile being kind and decent, especially to people who don't know you.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I agree mate - I mean kindness is its own reward but it also brings out the best in folks and Stuart certainly made me want to do better - and I don't think he even knew it - he is just a kind fellow
